≡(~q∧~p)

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given,

p∨q⇒~r∨p

≡~p∨q∨~r∨p

≡~p∧~q∨p∨~r

≡~p∨p∧~q∨p∨~r

≡~q∨p∨~r

So negation of~q∨p∨~r  is ~p∧q∧r.

subject: Mathematics
solution:

p q ~p ~p∧q p⇒q q⇒p p⇒q∨~p∧q q⇒p∨~p∧q
T T F F T T T T
T F F F F T F T
F T T T T F T T

Hence, q⇒p∨~p∧q is a tautology, and p⇒q∨~p∧q is neither tautology and contradiction,

So both statements are false.

subject: Mathematics
options: The match will not be played and weather is not good and ground is wet.
If the match will not be played, then either weather is not good or ground is wet.
The match will be played and weather is not good or ground is wet.
The match will not be played or weather is good and ground is not wet.
solution:
Let p: weather is good
and q: ground is not wet

Given statement "The match will be played only if the weather is good and ground is not wet" can be written as p∧q

Now, negation of  p∧q is ~p∧q≡~p∨~q

which is equivalent to "The match will be played and weather is not good or ground is wet".

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given,
p: Ramesh listens to music.
q: Ramesh is out of his village
r: It is Sunday
s: It is Saturday

Then the statement "Ramesh listens to music only if he is in his village and it is Sunday or Saturday" can be expressed as

Now on breaking each statement and combining them we get,

p≡ Ramesh listens to music

~q≡ He is in village.

r∨s≡ Saturday or Sunday

So, on combining all statement we get,

p⇒~q∧r∨s

solution:
Given,

p⇒q∨p⇒r

Now using the formula A⇒B≡~A∨B

We get, ~p∨q∨~p∨r

≡~p∨q∨r

≡p⇒q∨r, so option C is true.

Now solving option 1 we get,

 p∧~r⇒q

≡~p∧~r∨q

≡~p∨r∨q

≡~p∨r∨q

≡~p∨r∨~p∨q

≡p⇒q∨p⇒r, so option 1 is also true,

Now solving option 4 we get, 

p∧~q⇒r

≡~p∧~q∨r

≡~p∨q∨r

≡~p∨q∨r

≡~p∨q∨~p∨r

≡p⇒q∨p⇒r, so option 4 is also correct.

Hence option B is only wrong.

Option (2): says $(p \wedge r) \rightarrow(p \vee r)$

i.e. $(p \vee r) \rightarrow F$

Since, $r$ is false

Hence, $(p \wedge r)$ is false.

solution: $\sim(\sim p \vee q)=p \wedge \sim q$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)=\sim p \vee(q \rightarrow p) \\
& =\sim p \vee(\sim q \vee p) \quad \text { since } p \vee \sim p \text { is always true } \\
& =\sim p \vee p \vee q=p \rightarrow(p \vee q) .
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
options: If it rains, I go to school.
If it rains, I do not go to school.
If I go to school, it rains.
If I do not go to school, it rains.
solution:
Let p= It does not rain

q= I go to school

Contrapositive of if p  → q is if ∼q → ∼p

So, If it rains, I do not go to school.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
From De Morgan's Law,

~p∨q=~p∧~q

p→q=~p∧~q

~p∧q→p∨q

=~~p∧q∧~p∧~q

=~~p∧f

=~f=t, where f is contradiction and t is tautology.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
The given statement is p∧~q∨~p

Let us take A∩Bc∪Ac.

Now let us apply distributive law to the above statement.

≡A∪Ac∩Bc∪Ac

≡U∩Bc∪Ac

≡Bc∪Ac

Now the given statement p∧~q∨~p will be equivalent to ~q∨~p or ~p∨~q.

Hence the given statement is equivalent to ~p∨~q,

Now negation of given statement by demorgan's law will be,

~~p∨~q≡p∧q

subject: Mathematics
solution:
~p⇔~q∧q  =p⇔q∧q

Proof: Solving the given ~p⇔~q

=~p→~q∧~q→p

=~~p∨~q∧q∨p

=~~p∧q∧p∨q

=p∧q∨~p∨q      ...1

Now, p⇔q≡p→q∧q→p

=~p∨q∧~q∨p

=~p∨q∧~q∨~p∨q∧p

=~p∧~q∨q∧~q∨~p∧p∨q∧p

=~p∧~q∨c∨c∨q∧p where c is contradiction

=~p∧~q∨p∧q

=~p∨q∨p∧q        ...2

So from equation 1 & 2 we get

~p⇔~q∧q≡p⇔q∧q

∴  ~p⇔~q∧q is equivalent to p⇒q∧p.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Using logical equivalence condition p→q≡~p∨q, given statement can be expressed as, 

p∧~q→p→~q

≡ ~p∧~q∨~p∨~q.......(1)

We know De Morgan's law of logical equivalence is,

~p∧q≡~p∨~q

Applying De Morgan's law in equation(1) we get,

≡ ~p∨q∨~p∨~q   ∵~~p≡p

≡ ~p∨~p∨q∨~q

≡~p∨t≡t (tautology)

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Case-I

If Δ≡∇≡∧

p∧q→p∧q∧r

then it is false if r is false.

So not a tautology

Case-II

If Δ≡∇≡∨

p∨q→p∨q∨r≡ tautology

then p∨q∨r≡pΔr∨q

Case-III

If Δ=∨,∇=∧

then p∧q→p∨q∧r is not a tautology

when p→T,q→T,r→F

Case-IV

If Δ=∧,∇=∨

subject: Mathematics
solution: Since truth value of $p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)$ and $p \rightarrow(p \vee q)$ are same, hence $p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)$ is equivalent to $p \rightarrow(p \vee q)$.
